In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|12=draft}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=draft}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=draft}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|12=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|12=draft}}
{{Japanese Mythology}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{citation needed|Magatsuhi no Kami}} {{citation needed|Magatsuhi-no-kami}}

revision.text:
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[Category:remove from Pages of uncertain originality]][[Category:/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (for compatible browsers)]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神 (神道)|12=draft}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話|12=draft}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|12=draft}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|kamiumi|ja|神産み|lt=birth of deities|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=draft}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|misogi|ja|禊|12=draft}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉|12=draft}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|12=draft}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=draft}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=draft}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|12=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|12=draft}}

33 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:禍津日神 up to rev 99353892
revision.text:
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|イザナギの禊ぎによって生まれた神々（『古事記』に基づく） [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]] '''禍津日神'''（まがつひのかみ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]である<ref name="T">[[山北篤]] 『東洋神名事典』 [[新紀元社]] 349頁。</ref>。
== 概要 == 神名の「禍」（マガ）は「災厄」、「ツ」は上代語の格助詞「の」、「日」（ヒ）は「神霊」の意味で、「マガツヒ」の名義は「災厄の神霊」という意味になる<ref name="T" />。
[[神産み]]で、[[黄泉]]から帰った[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[禊]]を行って黄泉の[[穢れ]]を祓った時に生まれた神で、『[[古事記]]』では'''八十禍津日神'''（やそまがつひのかみ）と'''大禍津日神'''（おほまがつひのかみ）の二柱、『[[日本書紀]]』第五段第六の一書では'''八十枉津日神'''（やそまがつひのかみ）と'''枉津日神'''（まがつひのかみ）としている<ref name="T" />。これらの神は黄泉の穢れから生まれた神で、災厄を司る神とされている<ref name="T" />。神話では、禍津日神が生まれた後、その禍を直すために[[直毘神]]（なおびのかみ、神直毘神、大直毘神）二柱と[[伊豆能売]]が生まれている<ref name="Y">[[戸部民夫]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 新紀元社 265頁。</ref>。なお、『日本書紀』同段第十の一書ではイザナギが大綾津日神を吹き出したとしている<ref name="T" />。これが穢れから生まれたとの記述はないが、大綾は大禍と同じ意味であり、大禍津日神と同一神格と考えられている<ref name="T" />。
== 考証 == 伊邪那岐命と[[伊邪那美命]]の間の子に'''[[大屋毘古神]]'''（おおやびこのかみ）がいるが、これは「大綾」から「あ」が取れて「大屋」になったものとされ、大綾津日神（大禍津日神）と同一神格とされる<ref name="N">川口謙二 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[柏書房]] 97頁。</ref>。須佐之男命の子で、[[大国主の神話]]において大穴牟遅神（大国主）がその元に逃れてきた[[五十猛神|大屋毘古神]]とは別神格である<ref name="N" />。
また、[[本居宣長]]は、禍津日神を[[祓戸神]]の一柱である[[瀬織津姫|瀬織津比売神]]と同神としている<ref>[[薗田稔]]、茂木栄 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[学研ホールディングス|学研]] 46頁。</ref>。『中臣祓訓解』『倭姫命世記』『天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記』『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』は[[伊勢神宮内宮]]第一別宮の[[荒祭宮]]祭神の別名として[[瀬織津姫]]、八十禍津日神を記している。
== 信仰 == 後に、この神を祀ることで災厄から逃れられると考えられるようになり、厄除けの守護神として信仰されるようになった<ref name="Y" />。この場合、直毘神が一緒に祀られていることが多い<ref name="T" />。
== 復古神道における神学的位置 == 本居宣長は、禍津日神を悪神だと考えた<ref name="H">[[子安宣邦]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 [[ぺりかん社]] 101、102頁。</ref>。宣長によると禍津日神は人生における不合理さをもたらす原因だという<ref name="H" />。この世の中において、人の禍福は必ずしも合理的に人々にもたらされず、誠実に生きている人間が必ずしも幸福を享受し得ないのは、禍津日神の仕業だとした<ref name="H" />。「禍津日神の御心のあらびはしも、せむすべなく、いとも悲しきわざにぞありける」（『直毘霊』）と述べている<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 有朋堂文庫 12頁。</ref>。
一方、[[平田篤胤]]は禍津日神を善神だとした<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 205、206頁。</ref>。篤胤によると、禍津日神は[[須佐之男命]]の[[荒魂]]であるという<ref name="HS" />。全ての人間は、その心に禍津日神の[[分霊]]と[[直毘神]]（篤胤は[[天照大神]]の[[和魂]]としている）の分霊を授かっているのだという<ref name="HS" />。人間が[[悪]]や[[ケガレ]]に直面したとき、それらに対して怒り、憎しみ、荒々しく反応するのは、自らの心の中に禍津日神の分霊の働きによるものだとした<ref name="HS" />。つまり、悪を悪だと判断する人の心の働きを司る神だというのである<ref name="HS" />。またその怒りは直毘神の分霊の働きにより、やがて鎮められるとした<ref name="HS" />。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[瀬織津姫]] * [[日本の神の一覧]]
{{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まかつひのかみ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:天津神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:邪神・悪神]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[禍津日神]] to [[Magatsuhi-no-kami]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|イザナギの禊ぎによって生まれた神々（『古事記』に基づく） [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg SVGで表示（対応ブラウザのみ）]]] '''禍津日神'''（まがつひのかみ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]である<ref name="T">[[山北篤]] 『東洋神名事典』 [[新紀元社]] 349頁。</ref>。
== 概要 == 神名の「禍」（マガ）は「災厄」、「ツ」は上代語の格助詞「の」、「日」（ヒ）は「神霊」の意味で、「マガツヒ」の名義は「災厄の神霊」という意味になる<ref name="T" />。
[[神産み]]で、[[黄泉]]から帰った[[伊邪那岐命]]が[[禊]]を行って黄泉の[[穢れ]]を祓った時に生まれた神で、『[[古事記]]』では'''八十禍津日神'''（やそまがつひのかみ）と'''大禍津日神'''（おほまがつひのかみ）の二柱、『[[日本書紀]]』第五段第六の一書では'''八十枉津日神'''（やそまがつひのかみ）と'''枉津日神'''（まがつひのかみ）としている<ref name="T" />。これらの神は黄泉の穢れから生まれた神で、災厄を司る神とされている<ref name="T" />。神話では、禍津日神が生まれた後、その禍を直すために[[直毘神]]（なおびのかみ、神直毘神、大直毘神）二柱と[[伊豆能売]]が生まれている<ref name="Y">[[戸部民夫]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 新紀元社 265頁。</ref>。なお、『日本書紀』同段第十の一書ではイザナギが大綾津日神を吹き出したとしている<ref name="T" />。これが穢れから生まれたとの記述はないが、大綾は大禍と同じ意味であり、大禍津日神と同一神格と考えられている<ref name="T" />。
== 考証 == 伊邪那岐命と[[伊邪那美命]]の間の子に'''[[大屋毘古神]]'''（おおやびこのかみ）がいるが、これは「大綾」から「あ」が取れて「大屋」になったものとされ、大綾津日神（大禍津日神）と同一神格とされる<ref name="N">川口謙二 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[柏書房]] 97頁。</ref>。須佐之男命の子で、[[大国主の神話]]において大穴牟遅神（大国主）がその元に逃れてきた[[五十猛神|大屋毘古神]]とは別神格である<ref name="N" />。
また、[[本居宣長]]は、禍津日神を[[祓戸神]]の一柱である[[瀬織津姫|瀬織津比売神]]と同神としている<ref>[[薗田稔]]、茂木栄 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[学研ホールディングス|学研]] 46頁。</ref>。『中臣祓訓解』『倭姫命世記』『天照坐伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座次第記』『伊勢二所皇太神宮御鎮座伝記』は[[伊勢神宮内宮]]第一別宮の[[荒祭宮]]祭神の別名として[[瀬織津姫]]、八十禍津日神を記している。
== 信仰 == 後に、この神を祀ることで災厄から逃れられると考えられるようになり、厄除けの守護神として信仰されるようになった<ref name="Y" />。この場合、直毘神が一緒に祀られていることが多い<ref name="T" />。
== 復古神道における神学的位置 == 本居宣長は、禍津日神を悪神だと考えた<ref name="H">[[子安宣邦]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 [[ぺりかん社]] 101、102頁。</ref>。宣長によると禍津日神は人生における不合理さをもたらす原因だという<ref name="H" />。この世の中において、人の禍福は必ずしも合理的に人々にもたらされず、誠実に生きている人間が必ずしも幸福を享受し得ないのは、禍津日神の仕業だとした<ref name="H" />。「禍津日神の御心のあらびはしも、せむすべなく、いとも悲しきわざにぞありける」（『直毘霊』）と述べている<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 有朋堂文庫 12頁。</ref>。
一方、[[平田篤胤]]は禍津日神を善神だとした<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 205、206頁。</ref>。篤胤によると、禍津日神は[[須佐之男命]]の[[荒魂]]であるという<ref name="HS" />。全ての人間は、その心に禍津日神の[[分霊]]と[[直毘神]]（篤胤は[[天照大神]]の[[和魂]]としている）の分霊を授かっているのだという<ref name="HS" />。人間が[[悪]]や[[ケガレ]]に直面したとき、それらに対して怒り、憎しみ、荒々しく反応するのは、自らの心の中に禍津日神の分霊の働きによるものだとした<ref name="HS" />。つまり、悪を悪だと判断する人の心の働きを司る神だというのである<ref name="HS" />。またその怒りは直毘神の分霊の働きにより、やがて鎮められるとした<ref name="HS" />。
== 出典 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[瀬織津姫]] * [[日本の神の一覧]]
{{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:まかつひのかみ}} [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:天津神]] [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:邪神・悪神]]

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神 (神道)|12=draft|12=draft}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|12=draft|12=draft}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|kamiumi|ja|神産み|lt=birth of deities|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=draft|12=draft}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|misogi|ja|禊|12=draft|12=draft}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉|12=draft|12=draft}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft|12=draft}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|12=draft|12=draft}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=draft|12=draft}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|12=draft|12=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft|12=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=draft|12=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=draft|12=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft|12=draft}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|12=draft|12=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=draft|12=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=draft|12=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}}

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]

{{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (for compatible browsers)]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Malignant and Evil Deities]] [[Category:Evil deities]] }}
[[sn:Magatsuhi-no-kami]] [[en:Magatsuhi-no-kami]] [[en:Magatsuhi]] [[ja:禍津日神]]

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|kamiumi|ja|神産み|lt=birth of deities|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|kamiumi|ja|神産み|lt=birth of deities|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.

== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wgoodbye $1 7]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Malignant and Evil Deities]] [[Category:Evil deities]] }}
[[sn:Magatsuhi-no-kami]] [[en:Magatsuhi-no-kami]] [[en:Magatsuhi]] [[ja:禍津日神]]

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|deity|ja|神 (神道)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|ja|日本神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|kamiumi|ja|神産み|lt=birth of deities|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|misogi|ja|禊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|ja|黄泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoori Norinaga|qq=}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|qq=}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirata Atsutane|qq=}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|qq=}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|qq=}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />

revision.text:
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a [[:en:Kami|Deity]] appearing in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|qq=}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the [[:en:Kamiumi|birth of deities]], {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} performed a purification ritual ([[:en:Misogi|misogi]]) after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|qq=}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]] regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|qq=}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|qq=}} or gentle spirit of [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a [[:en:Kami|Deity]] appearing in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|qq=}}, p. 349.</ref>

These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|qq=}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]] regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|qq=}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|qq=}} or gentle spirit of [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a [[:en:Kami|Deity]] appearing in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|qq=}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the [[:en:Kamiumi|birth of deities]], {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|qq=}} performed a purification ritual ([[:en:Misogi|misogi]]) after returning from [[:en:Yomi|Yomi]] to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|qq=}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]] regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|qq=}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|qq=}} or gentle spirit of [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a [[:en:Kami|Deity]] appearing in [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]].<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|qq=}}, p. 349.</ref>

It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''[[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izanome|ja|伊豆能売|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar [[:en:Motoori Norinaga|Motoori Norinaga]] identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, [[:en:Hirata Atsutane|Hirata Atsutane]] regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂}} or gentle spirit of [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]]).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-Hime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-Hime|qq=}} * [[:en:List of Japanese deities|List of Japanese deities]]
