<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|1=Izanagi}}}} [[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Deity|1=Kami}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|1=Shinkigensha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503908}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|WD=Q3545159|lt=birth of deities|1=Kamiumi}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|Misogi|en|Misogi|WD=Q1936265|lt=misogi|1=Misogi}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491602|1=Q135491602}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />

[[ja:禍津日神]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:禍津日神]]
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification (based on the Kojiki) [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]]] '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神, まがつひのかみ) is a deity in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] 『東洋神名事典』 {{ill|Gakken|en|Gakken|WD=Q2664679|lt=Gakken|1=Gakken}} p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日) derives from "maga" (禍), meaning "calamity" or "misfortune," "tsu" (津), an ancient possessive particle, and "hi" (日), meaning "spirit" or "divine essence." The name signifies a "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|WD=Q3545159|lt=Birth of the Gods|1=Kamiumi}}, when {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} returned from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, he performed a purification ritual (禊) to cleanse the defilement of Yomi. As a result, the deities {{ill|Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja|八十禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} and {{ill|Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja|大禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} were born, according to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} refers to these deities as Yasomagatsuhi no Kami (八十枉津日神) and Magatsuhi no Kami (枉津日神).<ref name="T" /> These deities are considered to govern misfortunes and calamities.<ref name="T" /> After the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify and cleanse these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 New Current Books, p. 265.</ref> In another account from the Nihon Shoki, Izanagi expelled the deity Ōayatsuhiko no Kami, identified with Ōmagatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Interpretations == The deity {{ill|Oyabiko no Kami|ja|大屋毘古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko no Kami|1=Oyabiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935046}}, a child of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, is thought to be another manifestation of Ōmagatsuhi no Kami, with the name "Ōya" derived from "Ōaya" (大綾).<ref name="N">{{ill|Kenji Kawaguchi|en|Kenji Kawaguchi|WD=Q11477938|lt=Kenji Kawaguchi|1=Kenji Kawaguchi}} 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[Kashiwa Shobo]] p. 97.</ref> This Oyabiko no Kami is distinct from the deity of the same name in {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="N" />
{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Haraedo-no-Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神|1=Haraedo no Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588504}} deity {{ill|Seoritsuhime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Mogi]] 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[Gakken Holdings|Gakken]] p. 46.</ref> In several texts, including the {{ill|Chinju no Michi|ja|鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinju no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Magatsuhi no Kami is recorded as an alternate name for the deity of the {{ill|Ara-matsuri|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2859485}} at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
== Worship == Magatsuhi no Kami later became regarded as a protective deity against misfortune and was worshiped for warding off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> Often, this deity is enshrined alongside the Naobi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Position in Revival Shinto Theology == Motoori Norinaga interpreted Magatsuhi no Kami as a malevolent deity responsible for the irrational aspects of human existence, such as the unfair distribution of happiness and suffering.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 {{ill|Pelican Publishing|en|Pelican Publishing|WD=Q7161431|lt=ぺりかん社|1=Pelican Publishing}} pp. 101–102.</ref> He lamented this deity's role, describing it as "a sorrowful act beyond reason."<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 Yuhodo Bunko, p. 12.</ref>
In contrast, [[Hirata Atsutane]] viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 pp. 205–206.</ref> He associated Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}. According to Atsutane, all humans possess a fragment of Magatsuhi no Kami's essence, alongside the essence of the Naobi no Kami, which he identified with the {{ill|Wakamitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.<ref name="HS" /> He argued that Magatsuhi no Kami inspires anger and indignation against evil and impurities within humans, representing the moral conscience that discerns right from wrong.<ref name="HS" />

Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491602|1=Q135491602}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />

[[ja:禍津日神]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:禍津日神]]
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification (based on the Kojiki) [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]]] '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神, まがつひのかみ) is a deity in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] 『東洋神名事典』 {{ill|Gakken|en|Gakken|WD=Q2664679|lt=Gakken|1=Gakken}} p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日) derives from "maga" (禍), meaning "calamity" or "misfortune," "tsu" (津), an ancient possessive particle, and "hi" (日), meaning "spirit" or "divine essence." The name signifies a "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|WD=Q3545159|lt=Birth of the Gods|1=Kamiumi}}, when {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} returned from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, he performed a purification ritual (禊) to cleanse the defilement of Yomi. As a result, the deities {{ill|Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja|八十禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} and {{ill|Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja|大禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} were born, according to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} refers to these deities as Yasomagatsuhi no Kami (八十枉津日神) and Magatsuhi no Kami (枉津日神).<ref name="T" /> These deities are considered to govern misfortunes and calamities.<ref name="T" /> After the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify and cleanse these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 New Current Books, p. 265.</ref> In another account from the Nihon Shoki, Izanagi expelled the deity Ōayatsuhiko no Kami, identified with Ōmagatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Interpretations == The deity {{ill|Oyabiko no Kami|ja|大屋毘古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko no Kami|1=Oyabiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935046}}, a child of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, is thought to be another manifestation of Ōmagatsuhi no Kami, with the name "Ōya" derived from "Ōaya" (大綾).<ref name="N">{{ill|Kenji Kawaguchi|en|Kenji Kawaguchi|WD=Q11477938|lt=Kenji Kawaguchi|1=Kenji Kawaguchi}} 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[Kashiwa Shobo]] p. 97.</ref> This Oyabiko no Kami is distinct from the deity of the same name in {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="N" />
{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Haraedo-no-Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神|1=Haraedo no Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588504}} deity {{ill|Seoritsuhime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Mogi]] 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[Gakken Holdings|Gakken]] p. 46.</ref> In several texts, including the {{ill|Chinju no Michi|ja|鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinju no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Magatsuhi no Kami is recorded as an alternate name for the deity of the {{ill|Ara-matsuri|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2859485}} at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
== Worship == Magatsuhi no Kami later became regarded as a protective deity against misfortune and was worshiped for warding off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> Often, this deity is enshrined alongside the Naobi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Position in Revival Shinto Theology == Motoori Norinaga interpreted Magatsuhi no Kami as a malevolent deity responsible for the irrational aspects of human existence, such as the unfair distribution of happiness and suffering.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 {{ill|Pelican Publishing|en|Pelican Publishing|WD=Q7161431|lt=ぺりかん社|1=Pelican Publishing}} pp. 101–102.</ref> He lamented this deity's role, describing it as "a sorrowful act beyond reason."<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 Yuhodo Bunko, p. 12.</ref>
In contrast, [[Hirata Atsutane]] viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 pp. 205–206.</ref> He associated Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}. According to Atsutane, all humans possess a fragment of Magatsuhi no Kami's essence, alongside the essence of the Naobi no Kami, which he identified with the {{ill|Wakamitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.<ref name="HS" /> He argued that Magatsuhi no Kami inspires anger and indignation against evil and impurities within humans, representing the moral conscience that discerns right from wrong.<ref name="HS" />

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:禍津日神]] {{wikidata link|Q11591203}} [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Evil deities]] [[Category:Evil gods]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|1=Izanagi}}}} [[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Deity|1=Kami}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|1=Shinkigensha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503908}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|WD=Q3545159|lt=birth of deities|1=Kamiumi}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|Misogi|en|Misogi|WD=Q1936265|lt=misogi|1=Misogi}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491602|1=Q135491602}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />

[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification (based on the Kojiki) [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]]] '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神, まがつひのかみ) is a deity in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] 『東洋神名事典』 {{ill|Gakken|en|Gakken|WD=Q2664679|lt=Gakken|1=Gakken}} p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日) derives from "maga" (禍), meaning "calamity" or "misfortune," "tsu" (津), an ancient possessive particle, and "hi" (日), meaning "spirit" or "divine essence." The name signifies a "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|WD=Q3545159|lt=Birth of the Gods|1=Kamiumi}}, when {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} returned from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, he performed a purification ritual (禊) to cleanse the defilement of Yomi. As a result, the deities {{ill|Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja|八十禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} and {{ill|Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja|大禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} were born, according to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} refers to these deities as Yasomagatsuhi no Kami (八十枉津日神) and Magatsuhi no Kami (枉津日神).<ref name="T" /> These deities are considered to govern misfortunes and calamities.<ref name="T" /> After the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify and cleanse these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 New Current Books, p. 265.</ref> In another account from the Nihon Shoki, Izanagi expelled the deity Ōayatsuhiko no Kami, identified with Ōmagatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Interpretations == The deity {{ill|Oyabiko no Kami|ja|大屋毘古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko no Kami|1=Oyabiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935046}}, a child of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, is thought to be another manifestation of Ōmagatsuhi no Kami, with the name "Ōya" derived from "Ōaya" (大綾).<ref name="N">{{ill|Kenji Kawaguchi|en|Kenji Kawaguchi|WD=Q11477938|lt=Kenji Kawaguchi|1=Kenji Kawaguchi}} 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[Kashiwa Shobo]] p. 97.</ref> This Oyabiko no Kami is distinct from the deity of the same name in {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="N" />
{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Haraedo-no-Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神|1=Haraedo no Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588504}} deity {{ill|Seoritsuhime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Mogi]] 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[Gakken Holdings|Gakken]] p. 46.</ref> In several texts, including the {{ill|Chinju no Michi|ja|鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinju no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Magatsuhi no Kami is recorded as an alternate name for the deity of the {{ill|Ara-matsuri|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2859485}} at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
== Worship == Magatsuhi no Kami later became regarded as a protective deity against misfortune and was worshiped for warding off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> Often, this deity is enshrined alongside the Naobi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Position in Revival Shinto Theology == Motoori Norinaga interpreted Magatsuhi no Kami as a malevolent deity responsible for the irrational aspects of human existence, such as the unfair distribution of happiness and suffering.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 {{ill|Pelican Publishing|en|Pelican Publishing|WD=Q7161431|lt=ぺりかん社|1=Pelican Publishing}} pp. 101–102.</ref> He lamented this deity's role, describing it as "a sorrowful act beyond reason."<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 Yuhodo Bunko, p. 12.</ref>
In contrast, [[Hirata Atsutane]] viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 pp. 205–206.</ref> He associated Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}. According to Atsutane, all humans possess a fragment of Magatsuhi no Kami's essence, alongside the essence of the Naobi no Kami, which he identified with the {{ill|Wakamitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.<ref name="HS" /> He argued that Magatsuhi no Kami inspires anger and indignation against evil and impurities within humans, representing the moral conscience that discerns right from wrong.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsuhime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:禍津日神]] {{wikidata link|Q11591203}} [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Evil deities]] [[Category:Evil gods]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|WD=Q813858|1=Izanagi}}}} [[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|WD=Q524158|lt=Deity|1=Kami}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|1=Shinkigensha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503908}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|WD=Q3545159|lt=birth of deities|1=Kamiumi}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|Misogi|en|Misogi|WD=Q1936265|lt=misogi|1=Misogi}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135491602|1=Q135491602}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|WD=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|WD=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:禍津日神]]
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification (based on the Kojiki) [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]]] '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神, まがつひのかみ) is a deity in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] 『東洋神名事典』 {{ill|Gakken|en|Gakken|WD=Q2664679|lt=Gakken|1=Gakken}} p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日) derives from "maga" (禍), meaning "calamity" or "misfortune," "tsu" (津), an ancient possessive particle, and "hi" (日), meaning "spirit" or "divine essence." The name signifies a "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|WD=Q3545159|lt=Birth of the Gods|1=Kamiumi}}, when {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} returned from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|WD=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, he performed a purification ritual (禊) to cleanse the defilement of Yomi. As a result, the deities {{ill|Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja|八十禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} and {{ill|Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja|大禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} were born, according to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} refers to these deities as Yasomagatsuhi no Kami (八十枉津日神) and Magatsuhi no Kami (枉津日神).<ref name="T" /> These deities are considered to govern misfortunes and calamities.<ref name="T" /> After the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify and cleanse these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 New Current Books, p. 265.</ref> In another account from the Nihon Shoki, Izanagi expelled the deity Ōayatsuhiko no Kami, identified with Ōmagatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Interpretations == The deity {{ill|Oyabiko no Kami|ja|大屋毘古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko no Kami|1=Oyabiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935046}}, a child of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q682306}}, is thought to be another manifestation of Ōmagatsuhi no Kami, with the name "Ōya" derived from "Ōaya" (大綾).<ref name="N">{{ill|Kenji Kawaguchi|en|Kenji Kawaguchi|WD=Q11477938|lt=Kenji Kawaguchi|1=Kenji Kawaguchi}} 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[Kashiwa Shobo]] p. 97.</ref> This Oyabiko no Kami is distinct from the deity of the same name in {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="N" />
{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Haraedo-no-Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神|1=Haraedo no Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588504}} deity {{ill|Seoritsuhime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Mogi]] 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[Gakken Holdings|Gakken]] p. 46.</ref> In several texts, including the {{ill|Chinju no Michi|ja|鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinju no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Magatsuhi no Kami is recorded as an alternate name for the deity of the {{ill|Ara-matsuri|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2859485}} at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
== Worship == Magatsuhi no Kami later became regarded as a protective deity against misfortune and was worshiped for warding off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> Often, this deity is enshrined alongside the Naobi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Position in Revival Shinto Theology == Motoori Norinaga interpreted Magatsuhi no Kami as a malevolent deity responsible for the irrational aspects of human existence, such as the unfair distribution of happiness and suffering.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 {{ill|Pelican Publishing|en|Pelican Publishing|WD=Q7161431|lt=ぺりかん社|1=Pelican Publishing}} pp. 101–102.</ref> He lamented this deity's role, describing it as "a sorrowful act beyond reason."<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 Yuhodo Bunko, p. 12.</ref>
In contrast, [[Hirata Atsutane]] viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 pp. 205–206.</ref> He associated Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}. According to Atsutane, all humans possess a fragment of Magatsuhi no Kami's essence, alongside the essence of the Naobi no Kami, which he identified with the {{ill|Wakamitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q495568}} of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.<ref name="HS" /> He argued that Magatsuhi no Kami inspires anger and indignation against evil and impurities within humans, representing the moral conscience that discerns right from wrong.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{translated page|ja|禍津日神|version=99353892|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:禍津日神]]
<!--Magatsuhi-no-kami--><!--Magatsuhi-no-kami-->
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:禍津日神]] {{wikidata link|Q11591203}} [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Evil deities]] [[Category:Evil gods]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:禍津日神]][[zh:禍津日神]] {{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|1=Izanagi}}}} [[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|qid=Q524158|lt=Deity|1=Kami}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|1=Shinkigensha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11503908}}, p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日神) can be broken down as follows: "禍" (maga) means "calamity," "ツ" (tsu) is an ancient possessive particle meaning "of," and "日" (hi) signifies "divine spirit." Thus, the name "Magatsuhi" translates to "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|qid=Q3545159|lt=birth of deities|1=Kamiumi}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|Misogi|en|Misogi|qid=Q1936265|lt=misogi|1=Misogi}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|qid=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135491602|1=Q135491602}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|qid=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />

== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|qid=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}}
{{Japanese Mythology}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{translated page|ja|禍津日神|version=99353892|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:禍津日神]]
<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:禍津日神]]
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification (based on the Kojiki) [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]]] '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神, まがつひのかみ) is a deity in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] 『東洋神名事典』 {{ill|Gakken|en|Gakken|qid=Q2664679|lt=Gakken|1=Gakken}} p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日) derives from "maga" (禍), meaning "calamity" or "misfortune," "tsu" (津), an ancient possessive particle, and "hi" (日), meaning "spirit" or "divine essence." The name signifies a "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|qid=Q3545159|lt=Birth of the Gods|1=Kamiumi}}, when {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} returned from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|qid=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, he performed a purification ritual (禊) to cleanse the defilement of Yomi. As a result, the deities {{ill|Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja|八十禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} and {{ill|Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja|大禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} were born, according to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} refers to these deities as Yasomagatsuhi no Kami (八十枉津日神) and Magatsuhi no Kami (枉津日神).<ref name="T" /> These deities are considered to govern misfortunes and calamities.<ref name="T" /> After the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify and cleanse these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 New Current Books, p. 265.</ref> In another account from the Nihon Shoki, Izanagi expelled the deity Ōayatsuhiko no Kami, identified with Ōmagatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Interpretations == The deity {{ill|Oyabiko no Kami|ja|大屋毘古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko no Kami|1=Oyabiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10935046}}, a child of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}}, is thought to be another manifestation of Ōmagatsuhi no Kami, with the name "Ōya" derived from "Ōaya" (大綾).<ref name="N">{{ill|Kenji Kawaguchi|en|Kenji Kawaguchi|qid=Q11477938|lt=Kenji Kawaguchi|1=Kenji Kawaguchi}} 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[Kashiwa Shobo]] p. 97.</ref> This Oyabiko no Kami is distinct from the deity of the same name in {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="N" />
{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Haraedo-no-Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神|1=Haraedo no Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588504}} deity {{ill|Seoritsuhime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Mogi]] 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[Gakken Holdings|Gakken]] p. 46.</ref> In several texts, including the {{ill|Chinju no Michi|ja|鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinju no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Magatsuhi no Kami is recorded as an alternate name for the deity of the {{ill|Ara-matsuri|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2859485}} at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
== Worship == Magatsuhi no Kami later became regarded as a protective deity against misfortune and was worshiped for warding off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> Often, this deity is enshrined alongside the Naobi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Position in Revival Shinto Theology == Motoori Norinaga interpreted Magatsuhi no Kami as a malevolent deity responsible for the irrational aspects of human existence, such as the unfair distribution of happiness and suffering.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 {{ill|Pelican Publishing|en|Pelican Publishing|qid=Q7161431|lt=ぺりかん社|1=Pelican Publishing}} pp. 101–102.</ref> He lamented this deity's role, describing it as "a sorrowful act beyond reason."<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 Yuhodo Bunko, p. 12.</ref>
In contrast, [[Hirata Atsutane]] viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 pp. 205–206.</ref> He associated Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}. According to Atsutane, all humans possess a fragment of Magatsuhi no Kami's essence, alongside the essence of the Naobi no Kami, which he identified with the {{ill|Wakamitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.<ref name="HS" /> He argued that Magatsuhi no Kami inspires anger and indignation against evil and impurities within humans, representing the moral conscience that discerns right from wrong.<ref name="HS" />

{{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|parents={{ill|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|1=Izanagi}}}} [[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification ritual (based on the ''Kojiki'') [//upload.wikimedia.org/]]]
'''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神) is a {{ill|Kami|en|Kami|qid=Q524158|lt=Deity|1=Kami}} appearing in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] ''Dictionary of Eastern Deities'', {{ill|Shinkigensha|ja|新紀元社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinkigensha|1=Shinkigensha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11503908}}, p. 349.</ref>

During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|qid=Q3545159|lt=birth of deities|1=Kamiumi}}, {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} performed a purification ritual ({{ill|Misogi|en|Misogi|qid=Q1936265|lt=misogi|1=Misogi}}) after returning from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|qid=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}} to cleanse himself of defilement. It was during this ritual that the Magatsuhi deities were born. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}'', two deities are mentioned: '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十禍津日神) and '''Ōmagatsuhi no Kami''' (大禍津日神). In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}'', they appear as '''Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami''' (八十枉津日神) and '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (枉津日神).<ref name="T" />
These deities, born from the impurities of Yomi, are considered gods who govern disasters.<ref name="T" /> According to mythology, after the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (including Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] ''The Eight Million Gods: Profiles of Japanese Spirits,'' Shinkigensha, p. 265.</ref>
Additionally, in another version of the same chapter in the ''Nihon Shoki'', Izanagi is said to have exhaled the deity '''Ōayatsuhi no Kami''' (大綾津日神).<ref name="T" /> Although there is no explicit mention of this deity being born from defilement, the term "Ōaya" (大綾) shares the same meaning as "Ōmaga" (大禍), suggesting that Ōayatsuhi no Kami and Ōmagatsuhi no Kami may be considered the same deity.<ref name="T" />
== Analysis == One of the children of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}} is said to be '''Oyabiko no Kami''' (大屋毘古神). It is believed that this name evolved from "Ōaya" (大綾) with the omission of "a," linking Oyabiko no Kami to Ōayatsuhi no Kami (or Ōmagatsuhi no Kami) as the same divine entity.<ref name="N">Kenji Kawaguchi, ''Japanese Deity Interpretation Dictionary,'' Kashiwashobo, p. 97.</ref>
However, the Oyabiko no Kami referenced in the {{ill|myth of Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主の神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/myth of Ōkuninushi|qq=}}—whom Ōnamuji (Ōkuninushi) fled to—is considered a different deity.<ref name="N" />
Moreover, the scholar {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami as the same deity as {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Haraedo no Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Motegi]], ''Encyclopedia of Japanese Deities: Shinto Rituals and the Eight Million Gods,'' Gakken, p. 46.</ref> Various texts, including ''{{ill|Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja|中臣祓訓解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatomi no Harae Kunkai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135491602|1=Q135491602}}'', ''{{ill|Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|ja|倭姫命世記|1=Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime no Mikoto Seiki|qq=}}'', and shrine chronicles related to the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, refer to Seoritsu-hime and Yaso Magatsuhi no Kami as alternative names for the deity worshiped in {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-miya|qq=}}, the first auxiliary shrine of the Inner Shrine of Ise.
== Worship == Over time, Magatsuhi no Kami came to be worshipped as a protective deity to ward off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> In many cases, {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} is enshrined alongside Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Theological Position in Fukko Shintō == Motoori Norinaga viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as an evil deity.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]], ''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' Pelican Publishing, pp. 101–102.</ref> According to Norinaga, Magatsuhi no Kami is the cause of irrational misfortunes in life.<ref name="H" /> He argued that in the human world, happiness does not always correspond to one's virtues, and sincere individuals may not always find joy due to the influence of Magatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="H" /> In his work ''Naobi no Mitama'', Norinaga wrote, "The wrathful spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami brings inevitable sorrow."<ref>''Naobi no Mitama, Goyoku Gaigon,'' Yūhōdō Library, p. 12.</ref>
Conversely, {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|en|Hirata Atsutane|qid=Q2572749|lt=Hirata Atsutane|1=Hirata Atsutane}} regarded Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">''The World of Hirata Atsutane,'' pp. 205–206.</ref> According to Atsutane, Magatsuhi no Kami represents the {{ill|aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} (wild spirit) of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}.<ref name="HS" /> He believed that every human being possesses both the divided spirit of Magatsuhi no Kami and that of Naobi no Kami (whom Atsutane identified as the {{ill|nigimitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nigimitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} or gentle spirit of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}).<ref name="HS" />
When humans confront evil or defilement, their instinctive anger and harsh reactions are seen as manifestations of Magatsuhi no Kami's spirit within them.<ref name="HS" /> Thus, Magatsuhi no Kami governs the human capacity to recognize and judge evil. However, this anger is eventually soothed through the influence of Naobi no Kami's spirit.<ref name="HS" />
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Seoritsu-hime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}} * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|qid=Q3240847|lt=List of Japanese deities|1=List of Japanese deities}}
{{Japanese Mythology}} {{Shinto Sidebar}} {{translated page|ja|禍津日神|version=99353892|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[Image:Descendants of izanagi alone.svg|thumb|right|200px|Deities born from Izanagi's purification (based on the Kojiki) [//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d3/Creation_myths_of_Japan_5.svg View in SVG (supported browsers only)]]]] '''Magatsuhi no Kami''' (禍津日神, まがつひのかみ) is a deity in {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}.<ref name="T">[[Atsushi Yamakita]] 『東洋神名事典』 {{ill|Gakken|en|Gakken|qid=Q2664679|lt=Gakken|1=Gakken}} p. 349.</ref>
== Overview == The name "Magatsuhi" (禍津日) derives from "maga" (禍), meaning "calamity" or "misfortune," "tsu" (津), an ancient possessive particle, and "hi" (日), meaning "spirit" or "divine essence." The name signifies a "spirit of calamity."<ref name="T" />
During the {{ill|Kamiumi|en|Kamiumi|qid=Q3545159|lt=Birth of the Gods|1=Kamiumi}}, when {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナギ}} returned from {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|qid=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, he performed a purification ritual (禊) to cleanse the defilement of Yomi. As a result, the deities {{ill|Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja|八十禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasomagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} and {{ill|Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja|大禍津日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmagatsuhi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 禍津日神|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}} were born, according to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} refers to these deities as Yasomagatsuhi no Kami (八十枉津日神) and Magatsuhi no Kami (枉津日神).<ref name="T" /> These deities are considered to govern misfortunes and calamities.<ref name="T" /> After the birth of Magatsuhi no Kami, the deities {{ill|Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Ōnaobi no Kami|lt=Naobi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naobi no Kami|qq=}} (Kamunaobi no Kami and Ōnaobi no Kami) and {{ill|Izunome|ja|伊豆能売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izunome|qq=}} were born to rectify and cleanse these calamities.<ref name="Y">[[Tamio Tobe]] 『八百万の神々 日本の神霊たちのプロフィール』 New Current Books, p. 265.</ref> In another account from the Nihon Shoki, Izanagi expelled the deity Ōayatsuhiko no Kami, identified with Ōmagatsuhi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Interpretations == The deity {{ill|Oyabiko no Kami|ja|大屋毘古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyabiko no Kami|1=Oyabiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10935046}}, a child of Izanagi and {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イザナミ|1=Izanami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q682306}}, is thought to be another manifestation of Ōmagatsuhi no Kami, with the name "Ōya" derived from "Ōaya" (大綾).<ref name="N">{{ill|Kenji Kawaguchi|en|Kenji Kawaguchi|qid=Q11477938|lt=Kenji Kawaguchi|1=Kenji Kawaguchi}} 『日本の神様読み解き事典』 [[Kashiwa Shobo]] p. 97.</ref> This Oyabiko no Kami is distinct from the deity of the same name in {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref name="N" />
{{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} identified Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Haraedo-no-Kami|ja|祓戸神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haraedo-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祓戸大神|1=Haraedo no Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588504}} deity {{ill|Seoritsuhime|ja|瀬織津姫|1=Seoritsu-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsuhime|13=User:Immanuelle/Seoritsu-hime|qq=}}.<ref>[[Minoru Sonoda]], [[Sakae Mogi]] 『日本の神々の事典 神道祭祀と八百万の神々』 [[Gakken Holdings|Gakken]] p. 46.</ref> In several texts, including the {{ill|Chinju no Michi|ja|鎮座次第記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinju no Michi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Magatsuhi no Kami is recorded as an alternate name for the deity of the {{ill|Ara-matsuri|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-matsuri|1=Aramatsuri-no-Miya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2859485}} at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}.
== Worship == Magatsuhi no Kami later became regarded as a protective deity against misfortune and was worshiped for warding off disasters.<ref name="Y" /> Often, this deity is enshrined alongside the Naobi no Kami.<ref name="T" />
== Position in Revival Shinto Theology == Motoori Norinaga interpreted Magatsuhi no Kami as a malevolent deity responsible for the irrational aspects of human existence, such as the unfair distribution of happiness and suffering.<ref name="H">[[Nobukuni Koyasu]] 『平田篤胤の世界』 {{ill|Pelican Publishing|en|Pelican Publishing|qid=Q7161431|lt=ぺりかん社|1=Pelican Publishing}} pp. 101–102.</ref> He lamented this deity's role, describing it as "a sorrowful act beyond reason."<ref>『直毘霊 馭戎慨言』 Yuhodo Bunko, p. 12.</ref>
In contrast, [[Hirata Atsutane]] viewed Magatsuhi no Kami as a benevolent deity.<ref name="HS">『平田篤胤の世界』 pp. 205–206.</ref> He associated Magatsuhi no Kami with the {{ill|Aramitama|ja|荒魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} of {{ill|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}}. According to Atsutane, all humans possess a fragment of Magatsuhi no Kami's essence, alongside the essence of the Naobi no Kami, which he identified with the {{ill|Wakamitama|ja|和魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakamitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 荒魂・和魂|1=Mitama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q495568}} of {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}}.<ref name="HS" /> He argued that Magatsuhi no Kami inspires anger and indignation against evil and impurities within humans, representing the moral conscience that discerns right from wrong.<ref name="HS" />
